content:
**Light** is a form of energy that produces the sensation of vision.

• Light travels in straight lines (rectilinear propagation) at about **3 × 10⁸ m/s in air/vacuum** — the fastest speed in nature.
• Light needs **no medium**: it crosses the vacuum of space from the sun to us.
• When light meets a surface or a new medium it may be **reflected** (bounced back), **refracted** (bent while passing through), or **absorbed**.

This chapter covers two big ideas:
• **Refraction** — bending of light between media (and its everyday effects).
• **Spherical mirrors** — concave and convex mirrors, the images they form, and their uses; then the **prism**, dispersion and the rainbow.
explanation: Contrast with sound: light is not a mechanical wave — it travels through vacuum, while sound cannot. This contrast is a favourite one-mark question.
examTip: Keep reflection and refraction definitions ready in one line each — 'bounces back' vs 'bends while passing through'.

subtopic: 2. Refraction of Light and Its Effects
content:
**Refraction** is the bending of light when it passes **obliquely** from one transparent medium to another.

**Cause**: the **speed of light changes** between media — fastest in air, slower in water, slower still in glass.

**Rules of bending**
• Rarer → denser medium (air → water/glass): bends **TOWARDS the normal**; angle of refraction < angle of incidence.
• Denser → rarer (glass/water → air): bends **AWAY from the normal**.
• Along the normal (perpendicular incidence): **no bending** — the ray only slows down or speeds up.

**Everyday effects of refraction**
• A pencil/stick partly dipped in water appears **bent at the surface**.
• A pool or tank appears **shallower** than it is (apparent depth < real depth) — dangerous for divers.
• A coin at the bottom of a vessel appears **raised**; an invisible coin becomes visible when water is poured in.
• A fish appears closer to the surface than it really is — spear BELOW the image to hit it.
explanation: All four effects have one explanation: rays from the under-water object bend AWAY from the normal on leaving the water; the eye traces them straight back to a point higher than the object's true position.
examTip: In every refraction diagram, measure angles from the NORMAL, never from the surface — mislabelled angles lose the mark even with correct bending.

content:
For a ray crossing the boundary XY between two media at point O:

• **Incident ray** — the ray approaching the boundary (AO).
• **Point of incidence** — O, where the ray meets the boundary.
• **Normal** — the line N₁N₂ perpendicular to the boundary at O.
• **Refracted ray** — the bent ray in the second medium (OB).
• **Angle of incidence (i)** — between the incident ray and the normal.
• **Angle of refraction (r)** — between the refracted ray and the normal.

**Optically denser / rarer**
• An **optically denser** medium slows light more (glass is denser than water; water denser than air).
• Optical density is about the SPEED of light, not mass density — though for common substances the two go together.

Quick check: air → water gives r < i (towards normal); water → air gives r > i (away from normal).
explanation: The incident ray, the normal and the refracted ray all lie in the same plane — state this when asked for the 'laws of refraction' at this level.
examTip: Draw the normal as a DOTTED line and mark i and r with arcs touching it; examiners check the arc positions before anything else.

content:
A **spherical mirror** is a part of a hollow sphere with one polished, reflecting surface.

• **Concave mirror** — reflecting surface curved INWARD (like the inside of a spoon); converges light.
• **Convex mirror** — reflecting surface bulging OUTWARD; diverges light.

**Terms**
• **Pole (P)** — midpoint of the mirror's reflecting surface.
• **Centre of curvature (C)** — centre of the sphere of which the mirror is a part.
• **Radius of curvature (R)** — distance PC.
• **Principal axis** — straight line through P and C.
• **Principal focus (F)** — where rays parallel to the principal axis meet (concave: REAL, in front) or appear to come from (convex: VIRTUAL, behind) after reflection.
• **Focal length (f)** — distance PF; **f = R/2** (F is midway between P and C).
• **Aperture** — the width of the reflecting surface.
explanation: Find f of a concave mirror with the sun: focus sunlight to the smallest brightest spot on paper; the mirror-to-spot distance is f (the sun's rays arrive parallel). The hot spot may char the paper — energy concentrates at the focus.
examTip: If R is given, halve it for f before doing anything else (R = 30 cm ⇒ f = 15 cm) — and label both C and F on every ray diagram.

subtopic: 5. Images by Concave and Convex Mirrors; Uses
content:
**Convex mirror** — for ANY object position the image is **virtual, erect, diminished**, behind the mirror.
• Use: **rear-view/side mirrors** in vehicles — small images allow a wide field of view. (Caution: vehicles look farther than they are.)
• Also: security mirrors at shop corners and parking exits.

**Concave mirror** — image depends on the object's position:
• Object far away/beyond C → image **real, inverted, diminished**.
• Object within the focal length (between P and F) → image **virtual, erect, MAGNIFIED**.

**Uses of concave mirrors**
• **Shaving/make-up mirror** and **dentist's mirror** — face/tooth inside F gives an erect magnified image.
• **Torch, car headlight, searchlight** — bulb at the FOCUS sends out a parallel beam (mirror used in reverse).
• **Solar furnace/cooker reflector** — converges sunlight onto the vessel at its focus.
• Doctors' head mirrors (ENT) — converge light onto the throat/ear.
explanation: One-line logic: convex always diverges (so always small, erect, virtual); concave converges, so it can do everything — real or virtual, magnified or diminished — depending on where the object stands.
examTip: Match-the-columns on mirror uses is near-certain: convex ↔ rear-view; concave ↔ shaving, dentist, headlight, solar cooker.

content:
**Real image**
• Formed by the **actual meeting** of reflected (or refracted) rays.
• **Can be obtained on a screen**.
• Always **inverted**.
• Example: image of a distant building formed by a concave mirror; cinema screen image.

**Virtual image**
• Formed where rays only **appear to meet** when traced backwards.
• **Cannot** be caught on a screen — no light actually passes through it.
• Always **erect**.
• Examples: your image in a plane mirror; convex-mirror images; the magnified face in a shaving mirror.

**Three differences to quote**: actual vs apparent meeting of rays; screen vs no screen; inverted vs erect.
explanation: Why no screen can catch a virtual image: a screen works by scattering light that falls on it — at a virtual image position there is no light arriving at all.
examTip: 'Real ⇒ inverted, virtual ⇒ erect' holds for ALL mirror images at this level — a free consistency check for every answer.

content:
How the image changes as an object approaches a concave mirror:

• Object at **infinity** → image AT F: real, inverted, highly diminished (point-like).
• Object **beyond C** → image between C and F: real, inverted, diminished.
• Object **at C** → image at C: real, inverted, **same size**.
• Object **between C and F** → image beyond C: real, inverted, **magnified**.
• Object **at F** → reflected rays emerge **parallel**; image 'at infinity' (this is the torch/headlight arrangement run in reverse).
• Object **between P and F** → image behind the mirror: **virtual, erect, magnified** (shaving-mirror position).

**Pattern to remember**: as the object walks in from infinity, the real image walks OUT from F (growing all the time); the moment the object crosses inside F, the image flips behind the mirror and becomes virtual, erect and magnified.
explanation: Two rays are enough for any diagram: (1) a ray parallel to the axis reflects through F; (2) a ray through C returns along itself. Their intersection is the image point.
examTip: Learn the table as a story (object walks in, image walks out), not as six separate rows — you can then reconstruct any row in the exam.

subtopic: 8. The Prism: Dispersion and the Rainbow
content:
A **prism** is a transparent medium bounded by two plane refracting surfaces inclined at an angle.

**Dispersion**: white light passing through a prism splits into its **seven constituent colours** — **VIBGYOR** (Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red). The coloured band is called the **spectrum**.

**Why dispersion happens**
• Different colours travel at slightly **different speeds in glass**.
• Violet is slowed most → **deviates most**; red is slowed least → **deviates least**.
• The prism only **separates** the colours already present — it does not create or add colour.

**Why a glass slab shows no spectrum**: its two surfaces are PARALLEL, so the colours separated at the first surface are bent back parallel at the second and overlap into white again. The prism's INCLINED surfaces increase the separation instead.

**Rainbow**: millions of raindrops act as tiny prisms — sunlight is refracted, dispersed, internally reflected at the back of each drop and refracted again on leaving. Seen when the **sun is behind the observer** and rain in front.
explanation: Order of deviation (least → most): red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet — exactly the reverse of VIBGYOR read out loud. The deviation is always towards the BASE of the prism.
examTip: Two giveaway facts examiners test: red deviates least/violet most, and 'the prism does not create colours'.

content:
Two classic demonstrations prove that **white light is a mixture of seven colours**:

**1. The second (inverted) prism — Newton's experiment**
• A first prism disperses white light into VIBGYOR.
• An identical prism placed **upside-down** after it bends each colour by an equal and opposite amount.
• The colours **recombine** and emerge as white light.
• Conclusion: the prism separates colours; it does not manufacture them.

**2. Newton's colour disc**
• A disc painted with the seven rainbow colours in sectors.
• When rotated rapidly, it appears **nearly white**.
• The eye retains each colour impression for a fraction of a second (**persistence of vision**), so the rapidly succeeding colours blend into white — the reverse of dispersion, done inside the eye.
explanation: The disc never looks pure white because paints are impure pigments — 'whitish grey' is the honest observation, and stating that wins credit in practical-based questions.
examTip: Pair the two experiments in your answer: prism-2 recombines IN GLASS, Newton's disc recombines IN THE EYE.

subtopic: 1. Thermal Expansion in Solids and Its Applications
content:
On heating, a solid expands; on cooling it contracts. Molecules vibrate more vigorously and push slightly farther apart — the molecules themselves do not grow.

**Three kinds of expansion**
• **Linear** (length), **superficial** (area), **cubical** (volume).
• If α, β, γ are their coefficients: **β = 2α, γ = 3α** (α : β : γ = 1 : 2 : 3).
• Different solids expand DIFFERENTLY (brass more than iron); **INVAR** (Fe–Ni alloy) hardly expands — used for precision tapes and clock pendulums.

**Allowing for expansion**
• **Gaps between rails**; expansion joints in bridges and concrete roads; **bridge girders on rollers**.
• Electric/telephone wires strung **sagging** in summer so winter contraction cannot snap them.
• Small gaps at the ends of steel girders and around glass window panes.

**Using expansion deliberately**
• **Iron rim** heated, slipped on a cart wheel, cooled to grip (shrink fitting).
• **Red-hot rivets** hammered in; contraction clamps the plates.
• Hot water loosens a tight metal lid (metal expands more than glass).
• **Bimetallic strip** (two metals joined): bends on heating because they expand unequally — the working part of thermostats, electric irons, fire alarms.

**Problems caused by expansion**
• Thick glass cracks with boiling water (inner layer expands first, outer stays cool).
• A pendulum clock **loses time in summer** (longer rod → slower swing) and gains in winter.
explanation: Almost every application is one of three moves: leave room for expansion (gaps, rollers, sag), exploit contraction (rims, rivets, lids), or exploit UNEQUAL expansion (bimetallic strip, cracking glass).
examTip: For any 'give reason' on rails/wires/rivets, name the season and the direction of change (expands in summer / contracts in winter) — vague answers lose the mark.

subtopic: 2. Expansion of Liquids; the Water Anomaly
content:
A liquid has no shape of its own, so only **cubical (volume) expansion** is meaningful for it.

• Liquids generally expand MORE than solids for the same temperature rise.
• **Different liquids expand differently** (alcohol expands more than water).
• **Thermometer principle**: mercury expands regularly up a fine capillary as temperature rises.
• Flask-and-tube experiment: plunged into hot water, the liquid level first **dips** (the glass flask expands first, enlarging its capacity), then **rises steadily** as the liquid's larger expansion takes over.

**Anomalous expansion of water**
• From 0 °C to 4 °C water **CONTRACTS** on heating; above 4 °C it expands normally.
• So water has **maximum density at 4 °C** (1 g/cm³); ice is LESS dense (R.D. ≈ 0.9) because water **expands on freezing** (~1/11 of its volume).

**Consequences**
• **Ice floats** on water.
• **Ponds freeze only at the top**: 4 °C water sinks to the bottom; colder, lighter water rises and freezes at the surface; the ice sheet insulates the water below — **fish survive** the winter.
• **Water pipes burst** in severe winters; a sealed full bottle cracks in a freezer — freezing water expands with enormous force.
explanation: Anomalous = 'opposite to normal'. Keep the two ranges separate: 0→4 °C contracts (density rising), above 4 °C expands (density falling). The 4 °C point is the answer to almost every question on this topic.
examTip: The pond diagram — ice on top, 4 °C water at the bottom, fish swimming — is worth drawing even when not asked; it makes the explanation self-evident.

content:
Gases expand the **most** of the three states — their molecules are nearly free, so heating speeds them up and they push outward strongly.

• For the same temperature rise, **all gases expand nearly equally** (unlike solids and liquids, which are substance-specific) — because intermolecular forces are negligible in every gas.
• Expansion order for equal heating: **solid < liquid << gas**.

**Demonstration**: balloon stretched over an 'empty' (air-filled) flask standing in hot water — the balloon inflates as the trapped air expands; in cold water it collapses again.

**Everyday examples**
• A **hot-air balloon** rises: heated air expands, becomes less dense than the surrounding cold air, and floats upward.
• Car/bicycle **tyres can burst on hot days or long drives**: the enclosed air cannot expand freely, so its pressure climbs.
• Never throw 'empty' aerosol cans or sealed containers into fire — the trapped gas expands dangerously.
explanation: If the gas is FREE to expand, its volume grows (balloon); if it is CONFINED, its pressure grows instead (tyre). Spotting which case a question describes gives the answer immediately.
examTip: 'All gases expand equally, but liquids/solids each in their own way' — a favourite true/false; the reason is the negligible intermolecular force in gases.

subtopic: 1. How Sound Is Produced and How It Travels
content:
**Sound is produced by vibrating bodies** — a drum skin, a plucked string, a tuning fork, our vocal cords. Stop the vibration (touch the bell) and the sound stops.

**Sound needs a material medium** (solid, liquid or gas); it CANNOT travel through vacuum.
• **Bell-jar experiment**: as air is pumped out, the ringing fades and disappears although the hammer is still seen striking; readmitting air restores it. (We still SEE the hammer — light needs no medium.)
• Astronauts on the Moon must use radio — there is no air to carry their voices.

**Speed of sound**: fastest in solids, slower in liquids, slowest in gases.
• Steel ≈ 5000 m/s, water ≈ 1500 m/s, air ≈ **340 m/s**.
• Closely packed molecules pass vibrations on quickly — hence an ear pressed to a rail hears a distant train earlier and louder than through air.

**Lightning before thunder**: light arrives almost instantly (3 × 10⁸ m/s) while sound crawls at 340 m/s — every 3 s of delay ≈ 1 km of distance.
explanation: Sound travels as compressions and rarefactions of the medium's particles — the particles vibrate about their positions and hand the disturbance on; they do not travel with the wave.
examTip: Bell-jar question = two marks: one for 'sound fades as air is removed', one for 'hammer still visible ⇒ light needs no medium'.

subtopic: 2. Frequency, Pitch and the Audible Range
content:
**Describing a vibration**
• **Amplitude** — maximum displacement from the mean position.
• **Frequency (f)** — number of vibrations per second; SI unit **hertz (Hz)**.
• **Time period (T)** — time for one vibration; **T = 1/f** (reciprocals).

**Pitch (shrillness) depends on FREQUENCY**
• Higher frequency → shriller note; lower frequency → deeper note.
• A mosquito's whine is high-pitched (fast wing-beats); a lion's roar is deep but loud.
• **Strings**: shorter, tighter or thinner strings vibrate faster → higher pitch (guitar frets, tuning keys).
• **Air columns**: shorter column → higher pitch (flute holes, whistles).
• Women's and children's vocal cords are shorter and thinner → higher-pitched voices.

**Audible range of the human ear: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**
• Below 20 Hz: **infrasonic** (elephants, earthquake waves).
• Above 20 kHz: **ultrasonic** — heard by bats, dogs, dolphins; used in sonar and medical ultrasound. Bats navigate in darkness by **echolocation** with ultrasonic squeaks.
• A sound of a single fixed frequency is a **monotone** (tuning fork).
explanation: Pitch and loudness are independent: tightening a string raises pitch (frequency); plucking it harder raises loudness (amplitude) at the SAME pitch.
examTip: Memorise the trio: pitch↔frequency, loudness↔amplitude, T = 1/f — they decide nearly every MCQ in this chapter.

content:
**Wavelength (λ)** — the length of one complete wave: the distance between two consecutive compressions (or rarefactions).

**The wave equation**: **speed = frequency × wavelength**, v = f λ.

**Worked patterns**
• f = 500 Hz, λ = 0.68 m → v = 500 × 0.68 = **340 m/s** (speed of sound in air).
• 20 oscillations in 4 s → f = 5 Hz, T = 1/5 = 0.2 s.
• Thunder 6 s after the flash → distance = 340 × 6 = **2040 m ≈ 2 km**.

**Checklist**
• f in Hz, λ in m, v in m/s.
• Frequency is set by the SOURCE; it does not change as sound travels or with distance.
• In a new medium the SPEED and WAVELENGTH change, but the frequency stays the same.
explanation: Distance from a storm: count seconds between flash and thunder, multiply by ~340 m (≈ 1 km per 3 s) — the light's travel time is negligible.
examTip: Rearrange v = fλ before substituting (f = v/λ, λ = v/f); rearranging with numbers already inside is where slips happen.

content:
**Loudness depends mainly on AMPLITUDE** — in fact loudness ∝ (amplitude)². Strike a drum harder: larger amplitude, louder sound, SAME pitch.

**Other factors affecting the loudness heard**
• **Distance from the source** — the sound energy spreads over a growing area, so loudness falls with distance (frequency does not change).
• **Surface area of the vibrating body** — a tuning fork alone is faint; pressed on a table it sounds loud because the table-top sets far more air vibrating. Sounding boards of guitars, violins and pianos use this.
• **Density of the medium** — denser air (or wind blowing toward the listener) carries sound better.
• **Sensitivity of the listener's ear**.

**Measuring loudness**: the **decibel (dB)**.
• Whisper ≈ 30 dB, conversation ≈ 60 dB, heavy traffic ≈ 80–90 dB.
• Prolonged exposure above ~80 dB is **noise pollution** and can damage hearing.
• **Noise** is an unpleasant, irregular mixture of frequencies; a musical note is regular and pleasant.
explanation: Loudness ∝ amplitude² means doubling the amplitude makes the sound FOUR times as intense — the same square-law pattern as kinetic energy with speed.
examTip: List question 'factors affecting loudness' wants four: amplitude, distance, vibrating area, medium density — amplitude alone gets partial credit.

content:
• **Electric current** — the flow of electric charge through a conductor; measured in **amperes (A)** with an ammeter.
• **Potential difference (voltage)** — the 'electrical pressure' that drives current between two points; measured in **volts (V)** with a voltmeter. Indian mains supply ≈ **220 V**.
• **Resistance** — a conductor's opposition to the flow of current; measured in **ohms (Ω)**. Longer and thinner wires have more resistance; heating elements use high-resistance alloy (nichrome).
• **Conductors** allow current to pass easily: all metals (copper, aluminium), graphite, impure water, the human body.
• **Insulators** do not conduct: rubber, glass, dry wood, plastics, porcelain, dry air — used for wire coverings, plug tops, switchboards.

**Circuit basics**: a complete (closed) conducting path is needed for current to flow; a switch simply opens or closes this path.
explanation: Quantity–unit–instrument triplets to memorise: current–ampere–ammeter; potential difference–volt–voltmeter; resistance–ohm–(no instrument at this level); power–watt; energy–kWh.
examTip: Match-the-columns on quantity↔unit is almost guaranteed; write the five pairs once before the exam.

subtopic: 2. Power Rating and the Electricity Bill
content:
**Power rating**: every appliance is marked with its working voltage and power, e.g. **'220 V, 100 W'** — it consumes 100 joules per second when run on 220 V.

• Typical powers: LED bulb 5–10 W, CFL 15–20 W, filament bulb 60–100 W, fan ≈ 75 W, TV ≈ 100 W, iron ≈ 750 W, geyser/heater ≈ 1000–2000 W.
• Heating appliances (iron, geyser, heater) are the power-hungry ones.

**Electrical energy and the 'unit'**
• **Energy = power × time**.
• Commercial unit: **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** — 1 kW running for 1 hour. **1 kWh = 1 unit** on the meter = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.
• Cost = units consumed × rate per unit.

**Saving energy**
• Replace filament bulbs with **LEDs** — same light for about a tenth of the power (a filament bulb wastes most energy as heat).
• Switch off unused appliances; use ISI-marked, well-maintained equipment.
explanation: kW vs kWh: kilowatt is POWER (how fast energy is used); kilowatt-hour is ENERGY (how much was used). The bill charges for energy, never for power.
examTip: For cost numericals: convert W → kW, hours stay hours, multiply, then multiply by the rate — three steps, three marks.

content:
Electricity generated at power stations reaches homes through a supply network. The house connection has **three wires**:

• **Live wire** — carries current at high potential (**≈220 V**). Colour: **red** (old convention) / **brown** (new IEC convention). All switches and fuses go in THIS wire.
• **Neutral wire** — completes the circuit; at nearly **zero potential**. Colour: **black** / **blue**.
• **Earth wire** — connected to the metal body of appliances and to a **metal plate buried deep in moist soil**. Colour: **green** / **green-yellow**. It normally carries **NO current**; it conducts only when a fault leaks current to the body.

**At the meter board**: company fuse → **electricity meter** (records energy in kWh/'units') → main switch → distribution to room circuits.

In a **three-pin plug**, the thick top pin is the earth, and it is longer so the earth connects FIRST before live and neutral.
explanation: Why the earth pin is the longest and thickest: it must make contact before the live pin and offer the least resistance to any fault current — both pure safety design.
examTip: Quote BOTH colour conventions (red/black/green and brown/blue/green-yellow) — books differ, and naming both is always safe.

subtopic: 4. The Household Circuit: Fuse, MCB and Short Circuit
content:
**Electric fuse** — a deliberately weak point that protects the circuit.
• A **thin wire of low-melting-point alloy** (tin–lead) in a porcelain holder.
• Excess current heats the wire; it **melts and breaks the circuit** before the wiring overheats.
• Always placed in the **LIVE wire**, so a blown fuse disconnects the appliance from the high potential.
• Never replace a fuse with a thick copper wire — it will not melt, and the protection is lost.

**MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** — the modern fuse: an automatic switch that **trips** on excess current and can simply be reset (no wire to replace).

**Faults the fuse guards against**
• **Short circuit**: live and neutral touch directly (damaged insulation) — resistance becomes almost zero, current becomes enormous, wires heat within seconds → fire risk.
• **Overloading**: too many high-power appliances on one circuit draw more current than the wiring's safe rating.
explanation: Fuse rating logic: it must be slightly ABOVE the normal working current (so it doesn't blow in normal use) and well below the current the wiring can safely carry.
examTip: Three fuse facts earn full marks every time: thin wire, LOW melting point, connected in the LIVE wire.

subtopic: 5. Rules for the Safe Use of Electricity
content:
**At home**
• Never touch switches, sockets or appliances with **wet hands** (impure water conducts; wet skin has low resistance).
• Put switches in the **LIVE wire** — switched OFF must mean fully disconnected from 220 V.
• **Earth** all metal-bodied appliances (iron, geyser, fridge); use three-pin plugs.
• Never replace a fuse with thick wire; switch off the **mains** before any repair.
• Don't overload one socket with many high-power appliances; replace frayed/damaged wires; keep appliances away from water.
• Pull the **plug**, not the cord; never poke anything into a socket.

**During a thunderstorm (lightning safety)**
• Stay **indoors** or in a **closed car**; avoid open fields, terraces and swimming.
• NEVER shelter under a **tall isolated tree** — lightning prefers tall, isolated, conducting objects.
• Indoors, avoid wired phones and unplugging during the strike; tall buildings need a **lightning conductor**.

**If someone gets a shock**: do NOT touch them directly — switch off the mains or separate them with a dry wooden stick (insulator), then call for help.
explanation: Every rule is one physics fact in disguise: water/skin conduction, live-wire potential, the earth path, the fuse's melting point, or lightning's preference for tall conductors. Explain the physics and the rule explains itself.
examTip: 'Give two safety rules WITH reasons' — the reason carries the second mark; a rule alone gets half.

content:
**Static (frictional) electricity** — charges produced by rubbing two suitable bodies together.

**The mechanism: electron transfer**
• Only **electrons** move during charging; protons stay locked in the nucleus.
• The body that **loses electrons becomes positively charged**; the one that **gains electrons becomes negatively charged**.
• The two bodies always end up with **equal and opposite** charges — charge is transferred, never created or destroyed (**conservation of charge**).

**Standard pairs**
• Glass rod rubbed with silk → glass **positive**, silk negative.
• Ebonite/plastic rubbed with wool/fur → rod **negative**, wool positive.

**Everyday static**
• Crackling sparks while removing a synthetic sweater in the dark on a dry day.
• A comb run through dry hair picks up tiny paper bits.
• Dust clinging to a TV screen; a balloon sticking to a wall after rubbing on hair.
• Static builds best in **dry** weather; humid air leaks charge away.
explanation: Why two RUBBED bodies attract each other afterwards: they carry opposite charges (one lost the electrons the other gained). Why a charged body attracts an UNCHARGED one is a different mechanism — induction (see the next sections).
examTip: Always answer in terms of ELECTRON transfer — 'positive charge moved' is marked wrong at every level.

content:
**The two laws of electrostatics**
• **Like charges repel**; **unlike charges attract**.

**The sure test of charge is REPULSION, not attraction**
• A charged body attracts an UNCHARGED body too — it induces an opposite charge on the body's near side (the far side gets like charge, but it is farther away, so attraction wins). This is why a charged comb lifts neutral paper bits.
• Repulsion can only happen between two bodies with the SAME kind of charge — so only repulsion proves a body is charged.

**Lightning**
• Friction inside storm clouds builds enormous charges; when the air's insulation breaks down, a giant discharge — **lightning** — leaps between clouds or from cloud to earth. Thunder is the shock wave of the suddenly heated air.
• **Lightning conductor** (Benjamin Franklin): pointed copper spikes above the roof, joined by a thick copper strip to a **plate buried in the soil** — the discharge follows this easy path to the ground, sparing the building.
• Franklin's kite experiment showed lightning is electrical in nature.
explanation: Keep the two ideas linked: induction explains why attraction proves nothing, and that is exactly why repulsion is the conclusive test — one mechanism, two exam answers.
examTip: 'Why is repulsion the sure test?' — answer must mention induction-based attraction of uncharged bodies; that clause carries the mark.

content:
**Charging by conduction (contact)**
• The charged body **touches** the uncharged body.
• Charge flows across; the body acquires the **SAME kind** of charge.
• The charging body **loses** some of its charge.

**Charging by induction (no contact)**
• The charged body is only brought **NEAR**.
• Charges in the neutral body redistribute: the **near side acquires the OPPOSITE charge**, the far side the like charge.
• The charging body loses **no** charge.
• To charge permanently by induction: with the rod held near, **earth** the far side (touch it) to let the like charge escape, remove the finger, THEN remove the rod — the body is left with charge opposite to the rod's.

**Summary table**
• Contact: yes / no.
• Charge acquired: same kind / opposite kind (near side).
• Charging body's charge: decreases / unchanged.
explanation: Memory hook: CONduction = CONtact = same Charge; INduction = no contact = INverted (opposite) charge.
examTip: The three-row difference table (contact, kind of charge, loss of charge) is the standard 3-mark answer — learn it as a table.

content:
A **gold-leaf electroscope** detects and tests small electric charges.

**Construction**: a brass rod with a metal **cap** on top passes through an insulating stopper into a glass case; at the bottom hang two thin **gold leaves**.

**Detecting charge**
• Touch (or bring near) a charged body to the cap: charge reaches both leaves, they carry LIKE charge, **repel, and diverge**.
• **Greater charge → wider divergence** — so it also compares amounts of charge roughly.

**Identifying the NATURE of an unknown charge**
• First charge the electroscope with a KNOWN charge (say positive) — leaves diverge.
• Bring the unknown charged rod NEAR the cap (no touching):
  – Divergence **INCREASES** → the rod has the **SAME** charge (positive).
  – Divergence decreases → the rod is probably opposite (negative) — confirm by repeating with the electroscope charged negatively, because a decrease alone is not conclusive.
• Rule: only an **increase in divergence** is conclusive evidence.
explanation: Why the increase is conclusive: a like-charged rod pushes more like charge into the leaves (more divergence) — nothing else can cause that. A decrease could also be caused by an uncharged conductor or even a hand approaching.
examTip: State the conclusive rule ('increase in divergence is the sure indication') — it upgrades a description answer into a full-marks answer.

subtopic: 10. Solving Numericals: Electrical Energy and Cost
content:
**Formulas**
• Energy (kWh) = power (kW) × time (h)
• 1 kWh = 1 unit; 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
• Cost = units × rate per unit

**Worked patterns**
• 2 kW heater × 3 h = **6 units**/day; at ₹6/unit → ₹36.
• 100 W bulb × 10 h = 0.1 kW × 10 h = **1 unit**.
• 2000 W geyser × 30 min = 2 × 0.5 = **1 unit**; at ₹8/unit → ₹8.

**Checklist**
• Watts → kilowatts: divide by 1000.
• Minutes → hours: divide by 60.
• 'Units' on a bill ALWAYS means kWh.
• Monthly cost: multiply the daily units by the number of days first.
explanation: All errors here are unit errors: 100 W is 0.1 kW (not 100), and 30 minutes is 0.5 h (not 30). Convert both BEFORE multiplying.
examTip: Lay out: kW × h = kWh = units, then × rate = ₹ — one line per step; ICSE gives a mark for each correct line.
